title: Listening - Part 3: Work Experience
description: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about a work-experience placement they completed. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H what each speaker learned from the placement. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.
instructions: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about a work-experience placement they completed. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H what each speaker learned from the placement. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.

extracts.context: Five students talk about a work-experience placement.
extracts.audio_script: Speaker 1 Woman: I spent my placement in a primary school because I'd been considering teaching, although I wasn't certain. I helped children read and prepared materials for an art lesson. Of course, managing a lively class looked challenging. [19]But seeing a child understand a word she'd struggled with was so satisfying that, by the end of the week, I knew this was the kind of work I wanted to do.[/19] I've now applied for a course in education.||Speaker 2 Man: I worked in a café in the town centre. Carrying plates was easy enough, but on my second day a customer complained that his meal was cold and spoke quite sharply to me. I wanted to disappear. [20]The manager showed me how to listen politely, apologise and offer a solution without taking the criticism personally.[/20] Later I dealt with a similar complaint myself. It wasn't pleasant, but I was proud that I stayed calm and helped sort it out.||Speaker 3 Woman: My uncle arranged for me to spend a week in the warehouse where he works. I pictured people scanning parcels and driving small vehicles, and thought it might be fairly easy. [21]What I hadn't understood was how tiring it is to stand and walk for an entire shift while repeatedly lifting boxes and moving heavy containers.[/21] Everyone was friendly, but each evening I went straight to bed. I certainly have more respect for that kind of job now.||Speaker 4 Man: I wanted a placement in tourism and eventually found a travel agency that dealt with visitors to Spain and South America. I study Spanish at school, but I'd mostly used it in controlled classroom exercises. [22]At the agency I answered basic email enquiries in Spanish and even helped explain a ticket change to a caller, which was frightening but exciting.[/22] My grammar wasn't perfect, yet the staff said I'd communicated clearly. It gave my language lessons a real purpose.||Speaker 5 Woman: I've always liked drawing buildings, so I assumed working in an architect's office would suit me. The staff were kind and showed me their current projects. [23]However, I hadn't realised how much of the job involved revising tiny details on a screen, checking regulations and working to strict deadlines; after a week I knew I wouldn't enjoy doing that every day.[/23] I'm glad I discovered this now, because I can investigate other creative careers instead.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_1
dialogue.text: I spent my placement in a primary school because I'd been considering teaching, although I wasn't certain. I helped children read and prepared materials for an art lesson. Of course, managing a lively class looked challenging. But seeing a child understand a word she'd struggled with was so satisfying that, by the end of the week, I knew this was the kind of work I wanted to do. I've now applied for a course in education.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_2
dialogue.text: I worked in a café in the town centre. Carrying plates was easy enough, but on my second day a customer complained that his meal was cold and spoke quite sharply to me. I wanted to disappear. The manager showed me how to listen politely, apologise and offer a solution without taking the criticism personally. Later I dealt with a similar complaint myself. It wasn't pleasant, but I was proud that I stayed calm and helped sort it out.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_3
dialogue.text: My uncle arranged for me to spend a week in the warehouse where he works. I pictured people scanning parcels and driving small vehicles, and thought it might be fairly easy. What I hadn't understood was how tiring it is to stand and walk for an entire shift while repeatedly lifting boxes and moving heavy containers. Everyone was friendly, but each evening I went straight to bed. I certainly have more respect for that kind of job now.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_4
dialogue.text: I wanted a placement in tourism and eventually found a travel agency that dealt with visitors to Spain and South America. I study Spanish at school, but I'd mostly used it in controlled classroom exercises. At the agency I answered basic email enquiries in Spanish and even helped explain a ticket change to a caller, which was frightening but exciting. My grammar wasn't perfect, yet the staff said I'd communicated clearly. It gave my language lessons a real purpose.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_5
dialogue.text: I've always liked drawing buildings, so I assumed working in an architect's office would suit me. The staff were kind and showed me their current projects. However, I hadn't realised how much of the job involved revising tiny details on a screen, checking regulations and working to strict deadlines; after a week I knew I wouldn't enjoy doing that every day. I'm glad I discovered this now, because I can investigate other creative careers instead.

options.text: It confirmed their choice of future career.
It taught them how to deal with customers.
It allowed them to use a foreign language.
It showed them the value of teamwork.
It improved the way they organised their time.
It gave them experience of new technology.
It showed them how physically demanding a job can be.
It made them realise that a career was not for them.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: The experience in a primary school confirmed that the speaker wants to become a teacher (evidence [19]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: The placement taught the speaker how to respond appropriately to dissatisfied customers (evidence [20]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: The speaker discovered how much physical effort warehouse work requires (evidence [21]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: The agency placement enabled the speaker to use Spanish in real work situations (evidence [22]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: The placement led the speaker to decide against becoming an architect (evidence [23]).
